perturbation.original_text: IX. Injunctive Relief In the event of a breach or threatened breach or intended breach of this Non-Disclosure Agreement by Recipient , GTC, in addition to any other rights and remedies available to it at law o r
perturbation.changed_text: V. Use of Confidential Information The Recipient will not knowingly use Confidential Inf ormation for an illeg al or non - legitimate purpose . NDA Version 1 Initials: ________________ Page 3 of 7 Date : ________________ The Recipient of CEII may use CEII as foundation for advice provided to others, but may not disclose CEII to another individual unless that individual is an approved Recipient of the same CEII. The Recipient agrees that you will only use Confidential Information solely for the purpose as stated in Section II. You may not use Confidential Information for any other purpose. IX. Injunctive Relief In the event of a breach or threatened breach or intended breach of this Non-Disclosure Agreement by Recipient , GTC, in addition to any other rights and remedies available to it at law o r
perturbation.explanation: The injunctive relief clause has been moved to a section with similar information, and may not be seen in its own right.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia law requires contracts to be interpreted to give effect to the intent of the parties, but the displacement of the injunction remedy impacts a reader's ability to grasp the specific remedies available for a breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: Relocating the injunctive relief clause reduces its prominence. O.C.G.A. § 13-2-3 mandates contract interpretation to effectuate the parties' intent. By burying the clause outlining specific remedies, the modification obscures the remedies available upon breach, hindering a reader's full understanding of the agreement's consequences and thus potentially undermining its enforceability.

perturbation.justification: The relocation of the injunctive relief clause undermines the contract's enforceability by obscuring a critical remedy available to GTC in case of a breach. O.C.G.A. § 13-2-3 states that 'The cardinal rule of construction is to ascertain the intention of the parties.' By moving the clause, the intention to provide readily available injunctive relief is less clear, thus hindering the ability to enforce that remedy. The modified text makes it more difficult for a reader to fully understand the consequences of breaching the agreement, directly contradicting the principle of clear contractual intent.

perturbation.original_text: VIII. Return of Confidential Information GTC may revoke your access to Co nfidential Information at any time, with or without notice. Recipient shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information at GTC’s request or, at GTC’s option, certify destruction of the same within fifteen (15) days of a written request by GTC . Within such time period, each Recipient, if requested to do so, shall also submit to the GTC an affidavit stating that, to the best of his or her knowledge, all Confidential Information has been returned or destroyed and that Confidential Information no tes have either been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: II. Purpose Recipient desires access to information of a proprietary and confidential nature relating to (describe CEII and Confidential Information requested) __________________ NDA Version 1 Initials: ________________ Page 2 of 7 Date : ________________ ______ __________________ ________________________________ ______________ __ for the intend us e (describe): ____________________________ ____________ ________ ________________________________________________________________________ VIII. Return of Confidential Information GTC may revoke your access to Co nfidential Information at any time, with or without notice. Recipient shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information at GTC’s request or, at GTC’s option, certify destruction of the same within fifteen (15) days of a written request by GTC . Within such time period, each Recipient, if requested to do so, shall also submit to the GTC an affidavit stating that, to the best of his or her knowledge, all Confidential Information has been returned or destroyed and that Confidential Information no tes have either been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Placing the 'Return of Confidential Information' clause within the definition/purpose section, instead of its own distinct section reduces prominence and causes confusion for the reader.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Under Georgia law, contracts are to be construed in their entirety and in a manner that gives effect to each provision. By obscuring the 'Return of Confidential Information' clause, this is in contradiction of Georgia law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia law, specifically O.C.G.A. § 13-2-2(4), stipulates that a contract should be interpreted to give effect to all its parts. By moving the 'Return of Confidential Information' provision to a location where it does not belong will cause the party to overlook it. This clause becomes less noticeable and may be missed, which could undermine the intent to have information returned or destroyed, undermining a party’s clear understanding of their obligations
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 13-2-2 - Rules for interpretation of contracts generally :: 2010 Georgia Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Georgia Code 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code 2010 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Georgia Code TITLE 13 - CONTRACTS CHAPTER 2 - CONSTRUCTION § 13-2-2 - Rules for interpretation of contracts generally O.C.G.A. 13-2-2 (2010) 13-2-2. Rules for interpretation of contracts generally The following rules, among others, shall be used in arriving at the true interpretation of contracts: (1) Parol evidence is inadmissible to add to, take from, or vary a written contract. All the attendant and surrounding circumstances may be proved and, if there is an ambiguity, latent or patent, it may be explained; so, if only a part of a contract is reduced to writing (such as a note given in pursuance of a contract) and it is manifest that the writing was not intended to speak the whole contract, then parol evidence is admissible; (2) Words generally bear their usual and common signification; but technical words, words of art, or words used in a particular trade or business will be construed, generally, to be used in reference to this peculiar meaning. The local usage or understanding of a word may be proved in order to arrive at the meaning intended by the parties; (3) The custom of any business or trade shall be binding only when it is of such universal practice as to justify the conclusion that it became, by implication, a part of the contract, except in regard to those transactions covered by Title 11; (4) The construction which will uphold a contract in whole and in every part is to be preferred, and the whole contract should be looked to in arriving at the construction of any part; (5) If the construction is doubtful, that which goes most strongly against the party executing the instrument or undertaking the obligation is generally to be preferred; (6) The rules of gramma
perturbation.justification: The change in the contract involves relocating the 'Return of Confidential Information' clause to the 'Purpose' section, diminishing its prominence and potentially leading to it being overlooked. This contradicts Georgia law, specifically O.C.G.A. § 13-2-2(4), as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which states that a contract should be interpreted to give effect to all its parts. By obscuring the 'Return of Confidential Information' provision, the modification undermines the intent to ensure the return or destruction of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly references O.C.G.A. 13-2-2 and emphasizes that 'the construction which will uphold a contract in whole and in every part is to be preferred, and the whole contract should be looked to in arriving at the construction of any part.' Placing the return clause in an unexpected section makes it less likely to be noticed, thereby undermining the contract as a whole.
